caveats: a: The BLS occupation spans government, forestry, agriculture-support, and self-employment populations rather than NAICS 115310 alone.
a: No source measures wage-weighted AI exposure for this six-digit industry.

a: The injected labor-share input uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and a harmonization adjustment; that vintage mismatch affects the downstream labor opportunity even though it does not change this task share.
rho: The available evidence demonstrates technical feasibility and public-agency use, not five-year private-contractor implementation rates.
rho: Implementation potential differs sharply between consulting and inventory work and manual reforestation or suppression crews.
e: No observed study measures the eligible share of the frozen LMM firm population.
e: The frozen firm count is itself margin-bridged from receipts-size classes using a broad agriculture margin, which may misclassify forestry contractors.
e: Eligibility likely varies by subsegment because consulting, reforestation, pest control, and fire support have different recurrence and transferability.
s5: The proxy is national, all-industry, and based on 2018 responding employer-business owners.
s5: It measures owners rather than firms and does not observe sales, control changes, or transferability.
s5: Preseason agreements, licenses, safety records, and key customer relationships may not transfer cleanly.
q: No observed study of AI-benefit pass-through in forestry-support contracts was found.
q: Retention varies materially between fixed-price consulting, per-acre field services, emergency fire call-outs, and hourly work.
q: Federal and state customers can impose wage, reporting, and competition requirements that limit retention.
d5: Need, policy targets, and appropriations are not completed service quantity.
d5: Federal budget changes, litigation, permitting, fire-season variability, and workforce capacity can delay or cancel work.
d5: The BLS projection is an employment proxy and automation can lower employment even if service quantity rises.
o: The operator-required share is not directly observed.
o: Consulting and timber-inventory subsegments face more software substitution than reforestation, pest-control, and fire-support operations.
o: Public agencies can internalize work if staffing or procurement policy changes.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers include federal, state, Tribal, and local land managers, industrial and family forest owners, timber businesses, utilities, conservation organizations, and developers. Revenue commonly comes from competitively bid fixed-price or unit-price projects, time-and-materials consulting, multi-year or preseason blanket agreements, and repeat seasonal field programs.
lens.excluded_activities: Logging and timber ownership, government agencies performing work internally, captive forestry departments, one-person practices without transferable operations, equipment-only rental outside the industry, non-control investments, and shells.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms providing recurring or repeat outsourced forestry support to external customers, including timber cruising and estimation, forest management and consulting, reforestation and stand improvement, pest and vegetation control, wildfire prevention and suppression support, and related field implementation.

a.bridge: BLS describes forest and conservation workers across all employing industries rather than the complete wage-weighted occupation mix of NAICS 115310, while Forest Service tools document technology for planning rather than measured labor substitution at private contractors. I translate the occupation's mix of planting, clearing, measuring, spraying, fire suppression, equipment upkeep, communication, and supervision into a moderate exposed share concentrated in measurement, mapping, estimating, documentation, bids, scheduling, and analysis. The proxy likely overstates exposure for labor-heavy planting and fire crews but understates it for consulting, timber-cruising, and forest-management firms.
caveats: The BLS occupation spans government, forestry, agriculture-support, and self-employment populations rather than NAICS 115310 alone.
No source measures wage-weighted AI exposure for this six-digit industry.

The injected labor-share input uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and a harmonization adjustment; that vintage mismatch affects the downstream labor opportunity even though it does not change this task share.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit, wage-weighted occupation and task survey covering forestry consultants, cruisers, reforestation crews, pest-control operators, and fire contractors, with current software use and hours by task, would materially change the range.
a.rationale: Most field implementation is physical and safety-critical, but BLS explicitly notes that remote sensing lets fewer workers count and identify trees [S2]. Forest Service risk tools automate simulations, mapping, prioritization, and progress monitoring, reinforcing exposure in technical and administrative workflows [S6].

d5.bridge: The Forest Service backlog and treatment targets measure acres needing public-land reforestation and wildfire-risk treatment, while BLS projects employment for one forest-worker occupation across all industries; neither measures constant-price quantity for the full NAICS 115310 service basket. I translate the large unmet need and ten-year treatment agenda into modest five-year service-volume growth, tempered by BLS's 5% ten-year employment decline and its expectation that technology reduces labor per unit. This may overstate realized demand if appropriations, permitting, or contractor capacity constrain work, but may understate it if wildfire, insects, disease, and private-land programs accelerate.
caveats: Need, policy targets, and appropriations are not completed service quantity.
Federal budget changes, litigation, permitting, fire-season variability, and workforce capacity can delay or cancel work.
The BLS projection is an employment proxy and automation can lower employment even if service quantity rises.
d5.change_evidence: Deflated NAICS 115310 receipts or service-volume indexes, awarded contract acres and dollars by service type, appropriations and obligation schedules, timber-cruise volumes, and private-land treatment forecasts would materially change the ratio.
d5.rationale: The Forest Service identifies more than 4 million acres potentially needing reforestation and a historical capacity shortfall, while its wildfire strategy calls for up to 20 million additional National Forest acres and 30 million acres on other lands to be treated [S4][S5][S9]. BLS expects some wildfire-driven demand but a 5% decline in forest and conservation worker employment through 2034 as technology raises productivity [S2].

caveats: No observed study measures the eligible share of the frozen LMM firm population.
The frozen firm count is itself margin-bridged from receipts-size classes using a broad agriculture margin, which may misclassify forestry contractors.
Eligibility likely varies by subsegment because consulting, reforestation, pest control, and fire support have different recurrence and transferability.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level roster for the frozen LMM band showing ownership, customer and contract recurrence, service mix, normalized EBITDA, key-person dependency, and transferable qualifications would materially change the estimate.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition is fundamentally service-oriented, and Forest Service procurement shows repeat and multi-year external contracting structures [S1][S8]. Eligibility is reduced by episodic-only fire vendors, owner-dependent consultancies, captive or affiliated operations, nontransferable customer qualifications, and firms whose normalized EBITDA falls outside the lens despite the frozen size-band bridge.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 115310 ($1,591,915,454) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 115310 ($2,792,361,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.0804 (margins.json[11]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Farming/Agriculture EBITDA/Sales 8.04%); revenue/employee $178,894 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 115310); class <5: 1,330 firms, avg revenue/firm $533,150 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $42,865 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 244 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,540,205 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $123,832 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 99 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,308,485 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $185,602 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 138 firms, avg revenue/firm $5,857,717 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $470,960 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 22 firms, avg revenue/firm $24,755,136 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,990,313 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 8 firms, avg revenue/firm $15,743,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,265,737 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 30

caveats: The operator-required share is not directly observed.
Consulting and timber-inventory subsegments face more software substitution than reforestation, pest-control, and fire-support operations.
Public agencies can internalize work if staffing or procurement policy changes.
o.change_evidence: Customer insourcing studies, contract-line-item changes, adoption of autonomous field systems, remote-sensing substitution rates, and evidence that agencies accept software-only deliverables without field verification would materially change this estimate.
o.rationale: Year-five forestry work still needs accountable operators for field verification, prescribed treatments, planting, pest control, equipment, safety, crews, contract performance, and emergency response. Software and remote sensing can eliminate some manual cruising, counting, report preparation, and planning assignments, and customers may self-serve basic analytics, but they do not replace most physical execution or contractual accountability.

caveats: No observed study of AI-benefit pass-through in forestry-support contracts was found.
Retention varies materially between fixed-price consulting, per-acre field services, emergency fire call-outs, and hourly work.
Federal and state customers can impose wage, reporting, and competition requirements that limit retention.
q.change_evidence: A representative contract sample with pricing basis, escalation and audit clauses, renewal cadence, customer concentration, bid history, and post-improvement gross-margin retention would materially change this estimate.
q.rationale: Contractors can retain savings during fixed-price projects and between bid cycles, while productivity also supports capacity growth in constrained seasons. Competitive government solicitations, unit pricing, rebids, transparent specifications, and sophisticated industrial buyers are likely to recapture part of the benefit over five years. Time-and-materials and cost-reimbursable work retain less because lower billed hours can pass through directly.

caveats: The available evidence demonstrates technical feasibility and public-agency use, not five-year private-contractor implementation rates.
Implementation potential differs sharply between consulting and inventory work and manual reforestation or suppression crews.
rho.change_evidence: Matched before-and-after implementations from forestry software, drone and remote-sensing vendors and forestry contractors, reporting adoption, realized hours saved, error rates, field-verification requirements, and contract acceptance, would materially change this estimate.
rho.rationale: Remote sensing, GIS, fire-risk simulation, estimating assistance, automated reports, scheduling, and contract-document workflows are deployable within five years, and the Forest Service already uses analytical frameworks such as RiskMonitor [S6]. Implementation is constrained by remote connectivity, fragmented client systems, government deliverable requirements, safety and professional judgment, seasonal crews, data quality, and the continued need for field verification.

s5.bridge: The Census owner-age graphic covers responding owners of US employer businesses across all industries in 2018, not eligible LMM forestry-support firms. I use its finding that 51% of responding owners were age 55 or older as broad evidence of succession pressure, then discount substantially because age does not imply a control transaction and because closure, family succession, employee ownership, or continued ownership are alternatives. This likely overstates qualifying transfers if forestry firms are key-person dependent, but may understate them if the industry's rural owner base is older than the national employer-business population.
caveats: The proxy is national, all-industry, and based on 2018 responding employer-business owners.
It measures owners rather than firms and does not observe sales, control changes, or transferability.
Preseason agreements, licenses, safety records, and key customer relationships may not transfer cleanly.
s5.change_evidence: Owner-age and succession data for NAICS 115310, combined with five years of closed control transactions from business brokers, forestry lenders, insurance brokers, and public vendor ownership changes, would materially change the range.
s5.rationale: Broad employer-business demographics indicate meaningful owner aging, but there is no forestry-support-specific age, succession, or completed-deal series [S7]. A five-year qualifying-transfer probability materially below the older-owner share is therefore appropriate.

principal_driver: Large, persistent forest-restoration and wildfire-treatment needs paired with practical remote-sensing and workflow-automation opportunities.
principal_weakness: A small, estimated LMM firm pool exposed to public procurement, heterogeneous business models, and weak observed succession evidence.

sources.supports: Defines the industry as support related to timber production, wood technology, forestry economics and marketing, and forest protection, including timber estimating, firefighting, pest control, and reforestation consulting.
sources.title: 115310: Support Activities for Forestry

sources.supports: Describes physical forestry duties, reports that support activities for agriculture and forestry employ 23% of the occupation, projects a 5% employment decline from 2024 to 2034, and states that remote sensing allows fewer workers to count and identify trees while wildfire raises some demand.
sources.title: Forest and Conservation Workers

sources.supports: Documents that the available national occupation table is for the broader NAICS 115000 population and excludes self-employed workers, supporting the proxy limitation on occupational evidence.
sources.title: Support Activities for Agriculture and Forestry: May 2023 OEWS

sources.supports: Reports more than 4 million acres of potential National Forest reforestation need, identifies wildfire as about 80% of need, and describes the REPLANT Act and escalating disturbance drivers.
sources.title: Reforestation

sources.supports: Estimates that planting capacity fell about 3.8 million acres short of fire-driven need from 2000 through 2021 and anticipates the gap increasing by 2050.
sources.title: Mind the Gap: Reforestation Needs vs. Reforestation Capacity in the Western United States

sources.supports: Describes a Forest Service framework using simulations, maps, charts, and tabular summaries to prioritize fuel treatments, compare strategies, and monitor risk-reduction progress.
sources.title: RiskMonitor

sources.supports: Reports that 51% of responding owners of employer businesses were age 55 or older in the 2019 Annual Business Survey covering 2018.
sources.title: Business Owners' Ages

sources.supports: Documents preseason Incident Blanket Purchase Agreements, competitive vendor solicitations, and multiple five-year agreement periods for fire-support resources.
sources.title: Pacific Northwest Region Fire Procurement

sources.supports: States that the strategy calls for treating up to 20 million additional National Forest acres and up to 30 million additional acres on other federal, state, Tribal, and private lands.
sources.title: Science Supporting the Wildfire Crisis Strategy and Bipartisan Infrastructure Law

title: Support Activities for Forestry
